name: claude-toolkit
description: BeSpunky's Claude Code skills, subagents and commands. Develop once here; install and continuously upgrade across all projects.

plugins.name: bespunky
plugins.description: The BeSpunky toolkit front door. Its one skill, /bespunky:index, is a self-maintaining catalog: it lists every installed toolkit skill (grouped by plugin, each with its /bespunky-… invocation and a one-line 'use when') by reading the live available-skills set, so it never goes stale. All toolkit plugins share the bespunky- namespace prefix, so typing /bespunky in the slash menu filters the autocomplete to the whole toolkit at once — this index plus every skill.

plugins.name: bespunky-project-starter
plugins.description: Scaffold new BeSpunky-standard projects: integrated Nx monorepo + Angular (clean --minimal app, no demo) + devcontainer (Claude CLI & VS Code extension) + tailored CLAUDE.md + a per-window VSCode identity (bespunky-vscode-identity's window-identity generator, in the scaffold/--repair baseline). Generator-first. Scaffolded projects stay current on their own: a SessionStart hook orders the stamp in the project's HOUSE.md against the installed @bespunky/nx-tools and, when the toolkit has genuinely moved on, relays that fact so Claude can offer `scaffold.sh --repair` — which itself refuses to run without an explicit human yes.

plugins.name: bespunky-workflow
plugins.description: BeSpunky ways-of-working: the process, order, and methodology of how work moves from idea to production — independent of what's being built. branch-and-release: the house git methodology — a one-way promotion pipeline across four branches (feature → development → staging → main, each a strict ancestor of the next), unrelated work isolated in per-feature git worktrees off the integration branch (with the Nx-workspace-root override that stops tooling silently targeting the main tree), the relevance check that runs before touching any file, rebase-and-re-verify at the single divergence point (feature → development), and three human-gated promotions (feature done → development; go to staging; go live → main). local-server-isolation: launch test servers on a RANDOM free port (never the default/forwarded port a user's manual server owns) and never reap the user's running emulator suite. feature-package: a feature is a PACKAGE, not a scatter of files — one effort, one slug, one folder. The effort's slug (the one branch-and-release already picks for the branch feat/<slug> and worktree) is its IDENTITY, and its package is docs/features/<YYYY-MM-DD>-<slug>/ — born WITH the worktree, filled as the work happens, never written up at the end (a doc written afterwards is a memory, and memories are where the reasons go missing). Every skill that produces something durable writes INTO it instead of inventing a private home: the design quartet's BRIEF.md / VISION.md / STAGING.md / DECISION.md (distill-the-brief → envision-the-experience → stage-the-vision → mock-to-choose), the throwaway self-ignoring mocks/, and the effort's handoffs/ batons (session-handoff, located on resume by computing the newest package for the branch's slug — no committed pointer file, which would conflict on every promotion). Two rules keep it honest — the CONCLUSION is durable, the EVIDENCE is disposable (decisions and roads-not-taken are committed and permanent; mocks/scratch/spikes are self-ignoring, nothing may depend on them, and they're binned by default), and the user's OWN WORDS are the most valuable line in the package (quote the sentence that settled it; never paraphrase). The date prefix makes the trail chronological and lets a later pass at the same feature sit beside the first instead of overwriting it. Not a documentation ritual and not a required set of files — an effort writes only what it actually produced. It answers: six months on, why was it done this way, and what did we already rule out? session-handoff: carry a live effort across a context boundary into a fresh session — one skill, two modes (capture writes a distilled relay baton into the effort's feature package (docs/features/<date>-<slug>/handoffs/; resume LOCATES it by computing the newest package for the branch's slug — no committed pointer file, which would conflict on every promotion), resume re-grounds and picks it up), with the user's CORRECTIONS to Claude captured first-class and durable ones promoted to persistent memory so a lesson is never re-learned. Deploy bindings are a per-project parameter in the project's CLAUDE.md; the skills carry the durable method. Enabled by default in scaffolded projects.

plugins.name: bespunky-engineering
plugins.description: BeSpunky engineering skills. Agnostic foundation (architect-mentality + architecture-first: solve through design, never a patch; fix the root cause; design + confirm refactors) plus technique domains with progressive-disclosure references: software-design, advanced-typescript, angular-architecture, angular-native-wrappers, nx-monorepo-and-dx, resumable-state (every screen deep-linkable & refresh-safe — the URL as source of truth for navigational/view state, working state deliberately persisted to a chosen home), and typed-reactive-navigation (navigation is never a raw router call — typed per-domain navigation services that take entities not routes, URL-derived route-state selectors as the reactive read side, a domain event bus, and a pure event→command binding; partners resumable-state).

plugins.name: bespunky-design-system
plugins.description: BeSpunky design-system skills — styling as a SYSTEM, not a per-component afterthought. design-system-first: the DISCIPLINE (paired with an always-on directive the scaffolder bakes into every project) — go to the design system BEFORE building any feature UI; never hardcode a style value (every colour, space, radius, type step, elevation, border, duration and easing is a TOKEN — a CSS custom property consumed through the DS's SASS API; components read SEMANTIC tokens only, never a raw primitive); feature components COMPOSE DS components and tokens, they never invent appearance; the SECOND occurrence of a UI pattern is a PROMOTION into the design system (`nx g @bespunky/nx-tools:ds-component <name>` — one secondary entry point each), with BOTH sites migrated and the copies deleted, never a copy-paste; and when the DS lacks the concept you need, MODEL it (add the token, the semantic alias, the scale step, the component) — never a local override, an !important, a ::ng-deep reach-in, a duplicated token, or a one-off variant boolean. The DS is the single source of visual truth, so a re-theme or rebrand is a change of TOKENS, not a thousand component files. design-tokens-and-theming: the TECHNIQUE router — two layers, one truth (CSS custom properties = the runtime, themeable layer, where a mode is a re-binding through the cascade; the SASS API = the zero-output author-time layer) — over reference clusters: token taxonomy & naming (primitive → semantic → component); CSS custom properties as the runtime layer; the SASS API layer & how it's summoned (public barrel vs internal, and how it resolves in-repo vs published); theming & modes (light/dark/brand, where a mode lives, persisting it without a flash, contrast that holds in every mode); component styling & encapsulation (:host, ::part, ViewEncapsulation, the ::ng-deep ban, variants as data not booleans); the DS library's structure & entry points. Encodes the visual system stage-the-vision produces — it does not invent the look, it makes the look live in ONE place. Enabled by default in scaffolded projects.

plugins.name: bespunky-browser-automation
plugins.description: Two ways to drive a real browser. playwright: HEADLESS Playwright (Chromium, pre-installed) for solo automated verification, bug repro, before/after screenshots, DOM scraping, codegen. shared-browser: one in-container Chromium a human WATCHES via noVNC (forwarded :6080) while Claude drives it via loopback CDP — co-debugging and live in-place CSS/DOM verification with measured proof, with the fix ported to source and confirmed after reload. A decision tree keeps them distinct. Loaded by default in scaffolded projects.

plugins.name: bespunky-product-ux
plugins.description: BeSpunky product & UX design skills. distill-the-brief: the upstream FRAMING stage of the experience-design quartet - turn a messy request or dossier (docs, research, a product concept, a vague one-liner, a long thread) into the correct INPUT for envision-the-experience: the design problem(s), at the right altitude, with the solution stripped out, before any world/feeling/pixel. The core move - distil the PROBLEM, never the solution: read past the pre-baked answers a dossier carries ('a soft door held for one breath', 'a dashboard', 'a three-step wizard') to the real ache/intent/feeling/requirements underneath, and hand envision the problem so it can imagine the form - because a brief that smuggles in the solution turns visioning into transcription (the generic, off-the-feeling failure). Finds the altitude (too LOW = a whole solution; too HIGH = a vibe with no problem), keeps only the LOAD-BEARING constraints (a concept's twenty commandments drown envision - carry the few that would make a design wrong), and decides the GRAIN - what is one design problem (one envision-able, screenshot-able moment) vs many - splitting a plural intake into surfaces along real seams AND modelling the englobing CONTEXT they inhabit (the app/world/module is itself a design problem - its spine, world-rules, and how the surfaces compose and the whole recedes/bridges - framed WITH its children in mind so the split never fragments into unrelated designs). Produces a Brief Tree - an englobing context brief plus the surface briefs native to it, each a clean, solution-free envision-input (the design problem - who it's for and the ache - the feeling - the functional requirements as needs - only the load-bearing constraints - what is explicitly out, including the form). The new upstream member of the quartet distill-the-brief -> envision-the-experience -> stage-the-vision -> realize-the-vision (problem -> feeling -> art -> build); an expression of architect-mentality (build for the goal not the brief; model the missing concept - the englobing context designed for its children; refuse false tradeoffs; concentrate complexity so the edges stay simple). keep-users-oriented: whenever you make someone wait or move them through a process, answer the three questions — what is the expected result, where am I, what is the next step — and pick the right feedback for deterministic (steps/progress) vs nondeterministic (estimate + notify) processes. A universal service-design principle, expressed primarily through software UI (loading/progress, async, multi-step flows, long-running jobs, notifications, optimistic UI). astonishing-to-use: the co-equal force to the experience-design trio — a design must be astonishing to USE, not only to look at. The trio optimizes look and feel (the art); this optimizes the USE (UX): effortless, understandable, free of hoops, respectful of attention, a joy in the hand, given how people really hold devices and feel using them (thumbs, one-handed, distracted, tired, bad signal). The use and the look are two co-equal forces that refine each other in a PING-PONG, never a one-way check (concept↔ux, however many rounds); the mission sets who leads (utility → UX leads; brand/art → concept leads then pressure-tested) but it is always a loop, and you never ship great beauty with mediocre use. Hard bar: never below great UX (architect-mentality: refuse false tradeoffs — beautiful AND usable). keep-users-oriented is one facet. Tenets: respond instantly and like real life (direct manipulation follows the finger), never hang the UX (heavy work async/background), load instantaneously (no/short-live splash, lazy + sized placeholders), smooth transitions everywhere, avoid abrupt layout shifts (CLS) at all costs, action -> fitting reaction, lowest-friction input controls, facilitate input of each data type (even inventing a new control the world hasn't seen — our genius art), different layouts per device (not reflow), plan for device realities (the mobile keyboard). When validation or anything fails, be informative (what/why/how-to-fix — never just 'invalid') and carry the user to the problem (scroll into view / take-me-there, focus the control). A router (friction & flow; facilitating input; clarity & cognitive load; errors & recovery; responsiveness & perceived speed; continuity & transitions; embodied & contextual use; reconciling art & use; joy of use). redesign-means-rethink: the anti-anchoring entry gate — when asked to REDESIGN a UI, treat it as a complete creative reconception FROM SCRATCH, never a modification or reskin of existing code; the existing implementation has ZERO design authority (do not read it to inform the new design), reconceive the form from intent/requirements/feeling while honoring the purpose (what it is for, its data and functionality), and read the old code only AFTER the new design exists to plan cleanup/overwrite/migration — then a redesign runs the trio from scratch. A targeted tweak is NOT a redesign. envision-the-experience: the upstream visioning move — imagine the world an interface lives in (its feeling, place, metaphor) before any layout or component, and design every element as a native inhabitant of that world; the screen is a place, not a page. Interrogate every element the spec names (a 'menu' might become a sunflower whose petals you pick), speak in sensory non-technical language that names no implementation, hold immersive back from busy (restraint over spectacle) — then LAND the world on the app: anchor every inhabitant to the app's real anatomy (surfaces, flows, gestures, states), validate every transformed element against what the convention it replaced was buying (findability, scannability, thumb reach, safety) and against general app truths (one thumb, interruptions, long lists, empty/loading/error states), and produce a directable Vision (five beats incl. the anchor — the world-to-app map a stakeholder can point at and steer). These three form the experience-design trio (feeling → Staging → build), on one altitude ladder: sensory feeling → web-native art → engineering. stage-the-vision: the visual architect and creative MIDDLE — the Vision says what a screen should FEEL like but not what it should LOOK like, and grounding alone can't make ART (it only stops the generic/garish). This skill invents the bold, web-native artistic MOMENTS that turn the feeling into something you'd screenshot, staying at the ART level — it speaks the web's language (parallax, cinematic scroll, a character that turns to camera, a living background, type-as-image, reveal choreography) but describes what artful thing happens and how it feels, never how to build it (envision speaks pure feeling; stage speaks web-native art like 'a lion's face fills the hero and turns to meet your eyes as you scroll'; realize speaks engineering). Because a model isn't a native artist, it reaches art by inventing several BOLD concepts and choosing the striking-yet-true, STEALING from specific great work and adapting its moves, composing with craft (focal point, scale, negative space, the cinematic moment), and sourcing genuinely artful assets — grounded so it's not generic, restrained so it's not garish (bold != loud), and judged by an OUTSIDE EYE for beauty, never self-certified. Crucially it DECOMPOSES each moment to physical truth — light, shadow, material, colour, depth, form, texture as they really behave — and refuses the lone primitive that only symbolizes a phenomenon (warm light is never just a gradient; depth is never just a box-shadow). Produces the Staging (a bold concept + concrete described moments — each decomposed to physical truth and staged across form factors, mobile-first — + a visual system), confirmed at low fidelity before building (routing to references: the web as an art medium; inventing the concept; decompose to physical truth — the painter's physics; composition & cinematic staging incl. staging across form factors; stealing from great work; grounding, restraint & the taste gate). mock-to-choose: the DECISION instrument of the quartet — put the concepts in front of the user's EYES so they approve, reject, or course-correct in seconds, never by reading a long design doc (a written vision draws shrugs; a mock draws a verdict). The one move — build the CHEAPEST throwaway thing that makes the concept judgeable by eye, mock EVERY option on the table (one mock asks 'is this OK?' and gets a weak yes; three ask 'WHICH ONE?' and get a real answer), and put them side by side. A mock is SHELL AND PRESENTATION ONLY — layout, composition, palette, type, atmosphere, dressed with plausible dummy records and stand-in graphics so it reads like a real screen — with ZERO functionality (dead controls, no state, no routing, no data, no build step, no dependencies, no app integration). Heavy visual concepts (a scroll cinematic, a 3D scene, a generative background, physically-decomposed light) are NOT rendered but SUGGESTED with cheap stand-ins (one representative frame, a still, a flat approximation, a sourced photo), kept vivid enough that the atmosphere is unmistakable — the point is a fast verdict, not a faithful build; the two failure ends are OVER-rendering (the build, started without a decision) and UNDER-rendering (grey-box wireframes where all concepts look alike — the concept's one screenshot-worthy thing may never be cut). All variants share the SAME dummy content so the only difference the eye sees is the DESIGN. Every review runs on a shared HARNESS - a mini-app shipped with the skill (assets/mock-harness/: gallery + review layer + a random-port serve.sh) copied verbatim into the mocks folder, so Claude authors ONLY mocks.json (the question, what's faked, the variants + pitches) and one HTML file per concept - EVERY mock experience is therefore identical (same shell, same intent pins, same comment gesture, same read-back contract) and only the mocks change; never hand-roll a gallery, never edit the harness per project (a bug is fixed upstream in the asset). Because a bare low-fidelity mock reads as low QUALITY, every mock carries an INTENT LAYER - floating notes + hover popovers (Claude marks elements with data-note; a tiny inline kit renders numbered pins) that narrate the empty house the way an architect walks a site ('the sofa goes here, sideways, facing the window'; 'this dot is the light - it will float and breathe; here it's a static glow, so judge where it sits and how much of the frame it owns'), so the user judges the INTENT and never the shortcut - a note that is merely a label ('Hero section') explains away nothing. And because feedback on a look is all 'this' and 'that', the mocks support IN-PLACE COMMENTS: the harness serves a /comments endpoint, the user presses 'c' and clicks anything to pin a comment to it, and the server writes each to comments.json ON DISK - so Claude reads them back from a FILE (exact words, exact element, exact variant, exact viewport), which means an ASYNCHRONOUS review works as well as a co-driven one in the shared browser (noVNC); then reads them back, routes them UPSTREAM, and copies them VERBATIM into DECISION.md. The verdict is a real GATE not a poll: 'none of these' and 'a hybrid of A and B' are first-class outcomes (route upstream to re-conceive), and a mock YES is PROVISIONAL - it picks a direction, not the finished art (realize-the-vision still owes the outside-eye pass on the real result). Mocks live in a standard feature-and-date-scoped package (docs/features/<YYYY-MM-DD>-<slug>/mocks/ — inside the effort's feature package, the slug shared with the git branch) that is SELF-IGNORING and completely throwable — nothing outside it may ever depend on it, one rm erases every trace, the user may choose to keep any of it as a design record, and the DECISION is recorded durably (DECISION.md — what was chosen, in the user's own words why, what was rejected) so the conclusion outlives the evidence. Feedback goes UPSTREAM (re-conceive in stage-the-vision / envision-the-experience) and the mock is re-made cheaply — never polished into a prototype — and its code NEVER becomes the build (realize builds fresh from the confirmed Staging; every shortcut that makes a mock cheap is product debt if adopted). Routes to references (cheap stand-ins — the fidelity dial in practice; the mock package — layout, gallery, showing it, keep-vs-throw). An expression of architect-mentality (work smart not hard; know when not to build; design for the consumer — the user judges by eye) and architecture-first. realize-the-vision: the craftsman that turns a Vision AND a confirmed Staging into a real, running interface by RESEARCHING the truest, soundest means before writing any code — surveys the field (GSAP, Motion, three.js/R3F/angular-three, Web Animations, CSS scroll-driven animations, View Transitions, Lottie/Rive, Canvas/SVG/WebGL, Web Audio, haptics) and its caveats (jank, layout thrash, reduced-motion, accessibility, mobile), knows when to build in code vs. when to source an asset (figurative/photoreal art is generated, licensed, or downloaded — never hand-coded into path-soup — with licensing, review, and art-direction), requires a confirmed Staging before building (else invokes stage-the-vision first), engineers each staged moment, never self-certifies aesthetics, designs and confirms the architecture before building (architecture-first), and verifies against the feeling and the Staging in the running app — routing to a reference library of technique clusters. model-intent-not-data: model the user's INTENT on the way in and their DECISION on the way out — never mirror the system's data shape onto the screen. The SEMANTIC layer of input/output. On input, don't make the user COMPILE their intent into your schema (an AC remote asking for an action below a threshold and another above it, instead of a temperature RANGE + mode + power; a form exposing raw iCal freq/interval/byday; routing + account numbers instead of pick a payee). On output, don't make them DECOMPILE your data into a conclusion (an hourly temp/humidity table instead of 'umbrella after 3pm, grab a jacket'; a spend-by-category chart instead of 'on pace to overspend dining by $80, skip two takeouts'). The two tells — on input the user does arithmetic in their head to translate intent into the form; on output they stare and infer so-what. NOT always simplify — fidelity to the real task can COLLAPSE (two threshold rules → one range) or ENRICH (name the mode and power the schema omitted); model the concepts the task actually has. Decides WHAT concepts to collect and in what shape, and WHAT conclusion to deliver, then hands off to astonishing-to-use (facilitating-input) for WHICH control renders each value (best-not-common control, invented controls, finger/mouse/voice/phone). An expression of architect-mentality — model the missing concept, design for the consumer, concentrate complexity so the edges stay simple, build for the goal not the brief.

plugins.name: bespunky-voice
plugins.description: Hear Claude's questions and answer by voice, so you can work away from the screen. SPEAK: when Claude asks something, get a listenable, audio-dedicated summary — no markup or structure read aloud, just the gist and the options — on demand (`/voice say`; plus `/voice test`, `/voice status`) or automatically (`/voice auto on`, default off): a PreToolUse hook speaks multiple-choice questions and plan-approvals, and a Stop hook speaks plain free-text questions. ANSWER: `/voice answer` records your spoken reply, transcribes it locally, and hands it to Claude as your answer — no typing. HANDS-FREE: the `ask_by_voice` MCP tool + the `voice-conversation` skill let Claude ask aloud and BLOCK for your spoken answer with no command — say "let's talk by voice" and Claude routes its questions through it, matching your spoken choice (by label, ordinal, or yes/no) to an option. TTS is a swappable boundary (free espeak-ng floor on Linux / `say` on macOS, auto-upgraded to a local Piper neural voice via scripts/install-piper.sh, built to allow a cloud voice). STT is local whisper.cpp, opt-in via scripts/install-whisper.sh (base.en → small.en); listen.sh boosts the mic itself to beat WSLg's low input gain. Needs a synthesis engine, a player (paplay/aplay/afplay), Node, a reachable audio sink (WSLg's PULSE_SERVER in the BeSpunky devcontainer), and — to answer — a mic plus whisper.

plugins.name: bespunky-vscode-identity
plugins.description: Make every project's VSCode window recognizable at a glance — so multiple open windows stop looking identical, without Peacock's whole-window glow. window-identity: the skill finds the project's design-system primary colour and a project-fitting emoji, then hands them to the @bespunky/nx-tools:window-identity generator (the deterministic writer), which merges an emoji into window.title (dock / Alt-Tab / window switcher) and a quiet, design-system-derived band onto a bar (status by default; title bar or both optional). Colours are DERIVED, never hand-typed: from the design-system primary when one exists, else from a stable hash of the project name — so a window is distinct from birth, before the project has a design system, and the colour later SNAPS to the brand when the design system arrives. A provenance marker (.vscode/.window-identity.json) drives a no-clobber ratchet (name-hash < design-system < manual) so an upgrade never downgrades a design-system or hand-picked colour and never stomps a human's manual choice. A SessionStart hook detects — and only OFFERS, never runs — the name-hash → design-system re-derive once a project grows a design system. Committed team-wide by default; --personal keeps it a per-developer choice. Enabled by default in scaffolded projects; the generator runs in the scaffold/--repair baseline.
